The father is the mighty man — the archer — whose responsibility is to prepare the arrow and release it toward the target. The quality of the preparation determines the accuracy of the flight.

"As arrows" — arrows are shaped before they are shot. The shaft must be straight. The point must be sharp. The feathers must be properly aligned. All of this shaping happens in the father's hand — through discipline, instruction, encouragement, and example. A father who neglects the shaping process sends a crooked arrow into the world — one that veers off course and misses the target. A father who invests in the shaping process sends a straight, sharp, well-prepared arrow that flies true and hits the mark.

"In the hand of a mighty man" — the arrow's effectiveness depends on the strength and skill of the archer. A weak archer sends arrows that fall short. A strong archer sends arrows that reach distant targets. The father's spiritual strength — his faith, his character, his relationship with God — determines how far and how accurately his children's lives will travel. Investing in your own spiritual growth is not selfish; it is essential. You cannot launch your children farther than your own character can propel them.

OUTLINE POINT 3: Honor, Responsibility, and Restoration

"Children are an heritage of the LORD: and the fruit of the womb is his reward." Children are not burdens to be endured; they are rewards to be treasured. They are God's heritage — entrusted to fathers not as possessions but as stewardships. The father who views his children as God's heritage handles them with the care, respect, and intentionality that a sacred trust demands.

For fathers who have fallen short — and every father has — this lesson is also about restoration. The godly father is not the perfect father; he is the repentant father. He is the father who acknowledges his failures, asks for forgiveness, and commits to doing better. He is the father who does not let yesterday's mistakes define tomorrow's efforts. Grace is available for fathers who have been absent, harsh, neglectful, or spiritually passive. The cycle can be broken. The relationship can be restored. The arrow can still be straightened.

For those who are honoring fathers today — whether biological fathers, stepfathers, grandfathers, or spiritual fathers — let your honor be specific and sincere. Tell him what he did right. Thank him for the sacrifices he made. Acknowledge the strength of his character, even if it was imperfect. And for fathers who are no longer living, honor their memory by passing their best qualities to the next generation.

TEACHER EMPHASIS: Close this two-week Father's Day series with both challenge and encouragement. Challenge fathers to view their role with the seriousness it deserves — their integrity shapes generations, their strength determines how far their children travel, and their faithfulness creates channels of blessing that flow beyond their own lifetime. Encourage fathers who have failed to embrace restoration — it is never too late to become the father God intended. And invite the entire class to honor the godly fathers among them with specific, heartfelt appreciation.

APPLICATION: Write a tribute to your father or a father figure — specific, sincere, and personal. If your father is living, deliver it to him personally. If he has passed, read it aloud as an act of honor and gratitude. For fathers in the class: identify one area where you want to grow as a father and take one specific step this week.
discussionQuestions: Solomon says the just man's children are "blessed after him." How does a father's integrity create a channel for blessing to flow to his children?
The Psalmist compares children to arrows that must be shaped before they are launched. How are you preparing the "arrows" in your care for the target God has in mind?
The Psalmist says children are "an heritage of the LORD." How does viewing children as God's sacred trust change the way fathers approach their role?
For fathers who have fallen short: how can the principles of grace and restoration reshape your fatherhood going forward?
prayerFocus: Lord, we honor godly fathers today — men who walk in integrity, shape their children with wisdom, and launch them toward the purposes You have designed. Bless every father in this room. Where they have succeeded, strengthen them. Where they have failed, restore them. Where they have been absent, draw them back. Children are Your heritage — sacred arrows in the hands of mighty men. Make our fathers mighty in faith, strong in character, and faithful in their sacred responsibility. In Jesus' strengthening name, Amen.
weeklyAssignment: Write a tribute to your father or father figure — specific and personal. Deliver it if he is living; read it aloud if he has passed. Fathers: identify one growth area and take one step this week. Read Proverbs 20:7 and Psalm 127:3-5 daily. Journal your reflections on godly fatherhood. Share your tribute or growth step next Sunday.
